perturbation.original_text: CONSULTANT shall be liable for the disclosure of Confidential Information of <PAGE> NYNEX if such care is not used. The burden shall be upon CONSULTANT to show that such care, in fact, was used; and (e) Confidential Information is hereby acknowledged by CONSULTANT to be the sole property of NYNEX and shall be returned to NYNEX (including, without limitation, all materials, documents, drawings, models, apparatus, sketches, designs, specifications and lists, encompassing or evidencing same or related thereto, and all copies/formats thereof), within seven (7) days after receipt by CONSULTANT of a written request from NYNEX setting forth the Confidential Information to be returned. Upon receipt of such request, CONSULTANT also shall erase or destroy any such Confidential Information in any computer memory or data storage apparatus.
perturbation.changed_text: CONSULTANT shall be liable for the disclosure of Confidential Information of <PAGE> NYNEX if such care is not used; and (e) Confidential Information is hereby acknowledged by CONSULTANT to be the sole property of NYNEX.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the text requiring the consultant to return confidential information within seven days of a written request and the clause obligating the consultant to erase or destroy any confidential information in computer memory, the contract no longer provides adequate protection for NYNEX's confidential information and does not align with requirements of intellectual property protection under New York law, which is the governing law according to the document.
perturbation.law_explanation: N.Y. General Business Law § 350 prohibits deceptive acts and practices in the conduct of any business, trade or commerce or in the furnishing of any service in this state and requires businesses to ensure that data is adequately protected and returned upon request. By removing the explicit requirement for the consultant to return and destroy confidential information upon request, the modified contract creates a loophole where the consultant can retain and potentially misuse the information, conflicting with the spirit and intent of New York's consumer protection laws regarding business conduct and trade secrets. It also reduces the liability of the consultant in case of data breach which is a common practice that is frowned upon in New York.
perturbation.justification: The original contract included a requirement for the consultant to return and destroy confidential information within a specified timeframe, which aligns with protecting NYNEX's confidential information. The changed text removes this requirement. The Law Explanation suggests this omission conflicts with N.Y. General Business Law § 350 regarding deceptive acts and practices and data protection. Scraped Law Snippet 1 refers to Article 22-A of the General Business Law concerning consumer protection from deceptive acts and practices. While the snippet does not explicitly mention data return, the Perturbation Explanation argues that the omission fails to protect NYNEX's confidential information under New York law. The removal of the explicit return and destruction clause directly contradicts the spirit of protecting business data, making the contradiction clear and impactful.

perturbation.original_text: CONSULTANT warrants and represents that CONSULTANT has bound its employees, agents and subcontractors to the terms and conditions of this Agreement or that each and every employee, agent or subcontractor has personally executed a Non-Disclosure Agreement containing terms and conditions no less stringent than the terms and conditions contained herein; and furthermore CONSULTANT hereby <PAGE> agrees to indemnify, hold harmless and defend NYNEX from and against any loss, cost, damage, expense or claim arising out of any breach of this provision or the failure of CONSULTANT or its employees, agents or subcontractors to protect such Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: CONSULTANT warrants and represents that CONSULTANT has bound its employees, agents and subcontractors to the terms and conditions of this Agreement; and furthermore CONSULTANT hereby <PAGE> agrees to indemnify, hold harmless and defend NYNEX from and against any loss, cost, damage, expense or claim arising out of any breach of this provision or the failure of CONSULTANT or its employees, agents or subcontractors to protect such Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the phrase "or that each and every employee, agent or subcontractor has personally executed a Non-Disclosure Agreement containing terms and conditions no less stringent than the terms and conditions contained herein", the contract no longer ensures that all individuals with access to confidential information are explicitly bound by an NDA with terms as strict as the original agreement. This weakens NYNEX's protection of its confidential information and conflicts with New York law regarding trade secrets and confidential information.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New York Uniform Trade Secrets Act
perturbation.law_explanation: N.Y. C.P.L.R. § 4548 protects individuals who own data. Under the law, individuals who obtain confidential information have a duty to protect it. In the modification, this duty isn't contractually extended to every employee, agent or subcontractor because the clause that explicitly required them to sign an NDA, which ensures there's a direct obligation on their part to protect the information and provides NYNEX with a direct legal recourse against them, is removed.

Privileged communications; electronic communication thereof. No communication privileged under this article shall lose its privileged character for the sole reason that it is communicated by electronic means or because persons necessary for the delivery or facilitation of such electronic communication may have access to the content of the communication.
perturbation.justification: The omission of the clause requiring employees, agents, and subcontractors to execute Non-Disclosure Agreements (NDAs) weakens the protection of confidential information. The original contract ensured that everyone with access was bound by NDAs, providing NYNEX with direct legal recourse. The modified version removes this explicit requirement, creating a loophole where individuals handling confidential information may not be directly obligated to protect it contractually. Although Scraped Law Snippet 1, N.Y. C.P.L.R. § 4548, discusses privileged communications, it doesn't directly address the specific issue of NDAs for subcontractors. However, the removal of the NDA requirement creates a potential legal contradiction by failing to ensure the confidentiality of data, as the law explanation mentions individuals have a duty to protect confidential information. The contract modification weakens NYNEX's ability to enforce this duty contractually against all relevant parties.

perturbation.original_text: 3. The obligations set forth in Paragraph 2 hereof shall not apply, or shall terminate, with respect to any particular portion of NYNEX Confidential Information which: (a) was in CONSULTANT's possession, free of any obligation of confidence, prior to receipt from NYNEX, as proven by CONSULTANT's written records; provided, however, that CONSULTANT immediately informs NYNEX, in writing, to establish its prior possession; (b) is already in the public domain at the time NYNEX communicates it to CONSULTANT, or becomes available to the public through no breach of this Non-Disclosure Agreement by CONSULTANT; (c) is received independently from a third party free to disclose such information to CONSULTANT; (d) is developed by CONSULTANT, independently of and without reference to any Confidential Information of NYNEX or any other information that NYNEX has disclosed in confidence to any third party, as proven by CONSULTANT's written records; (e) is disclosed by CONSULTANT to a third party, with the express prior written permission of NYNEX; (f) is disclosed by CONSULTANT in order to satisfy any legal requirement of any competent government body; provided, however, that immediately upon CONSULTANT's receipt of any such request, CONSULTANT shall first advise NYNEX of same before making any disclosure to such body, so that NYNEX may either interpose an objection to such disclosure before such body, or take action to assure confidential handling of the Confidential Information by such body, or take other action to protect the Confidential Information which NYNEX deems appropriate under the circumstances; or (g) in any event, five (5) years after the date of execution of this Non-Disclosure Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: 3. The obligations set forth in Paragraph 2 hereof shall not apply, or shall terminate, with respect to any particular portion of NYNEX Confidential Information which: (a) was in CONSULTANT's possession, free of any obligation of confidence, prior to receipt from NYNEX, as proven by CONSULTANT's written records; (b) is already in the public domain at the time NYNEX communicates it to CONSULTANT, or becomes available to the public through no breach of this Non-Disclosure Agreement by CONSULTANT; (c) is received independently from a third party free to disclose such information to CONSULTANT; (d) is developed by CONSULTANT, independently of and without reference to any Confidential Information of NYNEX or any other information that NYNEX has disclosed in confidence to any third party, as proven by CONSULTANT's written records; (e) is disclosed by CONSULTANT to a third party, with the express prior written permission of NYNEX; (f) is disclosed by CONSULTANT in order to satisfy any legal requirement of any competent government body; or (g) in any event, five (5) years after the date of execution of this Non-Disclosure Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the clause "provided, however, that immediately upon CONSULTANT's receipt of any such request, CONSULTANT shall first advise NYNEX of same before making any disclosure to such body, so that NYNEX may either interpose an objection to such disclosure before such body, or take action to assure confidential handling of the Confidential Information by such body, or take other action to protect the Confidential Information which NYNEX deems appropriate under the circumstances;" eliminates the consultant's obligation to notify NYNEX before disclosing confidential information to a government body. This could conflict with NYNEX's legal rights to protect its trade secrets and confidential information and potentially violates New York law, which requires a good faith effort to protect confidential data before disclosure to a government body is made.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New York Civil Practice Law and Rules (CPLR) § 3101
perturbation.law_explanation: N.Y. C.P.L.R. § 3101 governs the scope of disclosure in civil actions. While it mandates disclosure of relevant information, it also recognizes the need to protect privileged or confidential information. By removing the requirement for the consultant to notify NYNEX before disclosing confidential information to a government body, the contract potentially undermines NYNEX's ability to assert privilege or seek a protective order under CPLR § 3101 to prevent or limit disclosure of sensitive trade secret information. This is against New York's civil procedure rules, making the consultant violate the code.

Scope of disclosure. (a) Generally. There shall be full disclosure of all matter material and necessary in the prosecution or defense of an action, regardless of the burden of proof, by: (1) a party, or the officer, director, member, agent or employee of a party; (2) a person who possessed a cause of action or defense asserted in the action; (3) a person about to depart from the state, or without the state, or residing at a greater distance from the place of trial than one hundred miles, or so sick or infirm as to afford reasonable grounds of belief that he or she will not be able to attend the trial, or a person authorized to practice medicine, dentistry or podiatry who has provided medical, dental or podiatric care or diagnosis to the party demanding disclosure, or who has been retained by such party as an expert witness; and (4) any other person, upon notice stating the circumstances or reasons such disclosure is sought or required. (b) Privileged matter. Upon objection by a person entitled to assert the privilege, privileged matter shall not be obtainable. (c) Attorney's work product. The work product of an attorney shall not be obtainable. (d) Trial preparation. 1. Experts. (i) Upon request, each party shall identify each person whom the party expects to call as an expert witness at trial and shall disclose in reasonable detail the subject matter on which each expert is expected to testify, the substance of the facts and opinions on which each expert is expected to testify, the qualifications of each expert witness and a summary of the grounds for each expert's opinion. However, where a party for good cause shown retains an expert an insufficient period of time before the commencement of trial to give appropriate notice thereof, the party shall not thereupon be precluded from introducing the expert's testimony at the trial solely on grounds of noncompliance with this paragraph.
perturbation.justification: The modified contract removes the consultant's obligation to notify NYNEX before disclosing confidential information to a government body. This contradicts the implicit obligation within N.Y. C.P.L.R. § 3101 to protect privileged or confidential information, as it undermines NYNEX's ability to assert privilege or seek a protective order. By removing the notification requirement, the change directly affects NYNEX's ability to protect its information, thus creating a legal contradiction.
